briefing.opening: Good morning. This is the climate change expert spokesperson briefing for March 25, 2026. We will now begin.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis Warnings and Reality
sections.content: Recent research indicates that even with a 2℃ rise in global temperature, extreme weather events will occur, and the risks of drought and heavy rainfall are projected to be greater than anticipated [1, 41, 55, 77]. Warnings have been issued both domestically and internationally that the climate crisis is approaching as a 'deeper collapse beyond prediction' [2, 48, 57, 67], and experts have stated that the late March heatwave would have been "virtually impossible" without climate change [53]. Domestic damages such as wildfires covering an area equivalent to 140,000 soccer fields and the worst drought in 108 years have already been reported [3], and globally, the combination of the La Niña phenomenon and climate change is causing flooding in areas like Hawaii [54]. The Amazon's dung beetles are threatened by climate change [47], and 'shocking' changes have been observed in a 2-degree warming experiment on Rocky Mountain wildflower meadows [75], confirming widespread impacts across ecosystems. This situation suggests that climate change is pushing our world into 'uncharted territory,' and there are reports that heatwaves could become the leading cause of death from climate change, surpassing floods [68].

sections.title: 🌍 Challenges in International Cooperation and Accountability
sections.content: Global climate cooperation faces significant challenges. In particular, China's climate envoy criticized the U.S. withdrawal from the Paris Agreement for weakening global climate cooperation [9, 10, 21]. Meanwhile, Maryland's highest court dismissed climate change lawsuits filed against oil companies [44, 56, 60], illustrating the difficulties in legal battles over accountability for climate change damages. New research indicates that the United States has caused an estimated $10 trillion in global climate damages since 1990 through massive greenhouse gas emissions, with a significant portion of this returning as economic hardship to the U.S. itself [74]. A study finding that climate change led to the collapse of an ancient Chinese civilization 4,500 years ago serves as a reminder of climate's long-term impact on human civilization [59], and discussions on the 'intricacy' of current climate action are also active [50]. Against this backdrop, India and the Church of England are taking climate action in their own ways, with India setting clean energy targets and the Church of England developing a climate change voting strategy targeting banks [65, 71].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic Carbon Neutrality Practices and Policy Trends
sections.content: Domestically, various entities continue their efforts to achieve carbon neutrality. Hyundai Steel and LS Electric were recognized and awarded for their contributions to climate change response and leadership in carbon neutrality [8, 13], and several companies and institutions are conducting campaigns to expand carbon-neutral infrastructure and promote ESG management [11, 12, 15]. Local governments are also actively contributing to the spread of a carbon-neutral lifestyle. Gunsan City is promoting carbon neutrality through citizen-participatory renewable energy transition [16], Haenam County through the implementation of a vehicle odd-even system [18], and Gangbuk-gu through environmental education utilizing smart farms [19]. Notably, Paju City demonstrated its commitment to becoming a leading global carbon-neutral city by joining 'GCoM,' the world's largest climate pact [22]. Legislative efforts are also becoming visible, with Representative Park Ji-hye proposing the 'Special Act on Fostering Carbon-Neutral Industries' to address the climate crisis and create new growth engines [17]. These multifaceted efforts demonstrate a strong will and commitment to achieving carbon neutrality goals spreading across Korean society [14, 20].

sections.title: 🌱 Acceleration of Renewable Energy and Green Technology Innovation
sections.content: In the renewable energy sector, innovative technology development and infrastructure construction are active. Samsung C&T completed Korea's first 100% renewable energy-based off-grid green hydrogen production facility, showcasing a 10MW solar-linked system [28, 30, 33, 34, 37, 40]. In Gimcheon, the nation's largest green hydrogen production has also begun, presenting the feasibility of a 100% renewable energy model through the commercialization of 'grid-independent green hydrogen' [23, 26]. The Taean offshore wind power project is progressing faster than the national average [32], and Korea Southern Power plans to develop wind turbine inspection technology using drones and AI [38]. AI technology is expected to slow climate change by supporting smart city design, not only in the renewable energy sector but also in urban planning [31, 35, 36, 63]. Local governments are also making efforts to expand renewable energy at the regional level, including promoting renewable energy housing support projects [27] and recruiting companies to participate in convergence projects [39], and expectations for income generation through renewable energy in rural and fishing communities are rising [24, 29].

sections.title: 📰 Climate Change Scientific Research and Policy Deliberation
sections.content: Research to broaden scientific understanding of climate change is steadily progressing. Glacier core research at Oregon State University (OSU) has revealed additional information about climate change [43], and studies have also shown that climate change is altering Sahara Desert dust and affecting Europe [49]. Interestingly, research has also been published indicating that climate change is slowing the Earth's rotation, a disturbance witnessed for the first time in 3.6 million years, which could even affect our everyday devices [72]. However, the指摘 that gaps in precipitation observation networks limit climate change impact assessment [42] emphasizes the need to expand infrastructure for accurate data-driven analysis. On the policy front, meticulous review and academic participation are required for the draft climate change plan for 2026-2040 [66, 73], which will play a crucial role in setting the long-term direction of climate action. Specific efforts for climate change adaptation are also continuing, such as Pyeongchang County's large-scale investment in river maintenance to proactively respond to climate change [4].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlighted the severity of the climate crisis, the international community's responsibility to overcome it, and the efforts in carbon neutrality and renewable energy technology innovation underway both domestically and internationally. We reaffirm that climate change is a complex challenge requiring a multifaceted approach and continuous action, and we expect these efforts to accelerate in the future. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: US has caused $10tn worth of climate damage since 1990, research finds
articles.description: US, top carbon emitter in history, has ‘a lot of responsibility’ for causing ‘substantial’ harm globally, scientist saysThe US has caused an eye-watering $10tn in global damages to the world over the past three decades through its vast planet-heating emissions, with a quarter of this economic pain inflicted upon itself, new research has found.By being the largest carbon emitter in history, the US has caused greater harm to worldwide economic growth than any other country, ahead of China, now the

summary.phenomenon: According to new research, the United States has, over the past three decades since 1990, through its vast greenhouse gas emissions, caused climate damages amounting to $10 trillion globally, with a quarter of this economic burden impacting the U.S. itself.
summary.cause: The United States, as the largest carbon emitter in history, and its vast planet-heating emissions are the cause of these damages.
summary.outlook: The United States has caused greater harm to worldwide economic growth than any other country, surpassing China.

articles.original_title: ‘It’s like flowers on steroids’: what happened when scientists heated a Rocky Mountain wildlife meadow by 2C?
articles.description: A long-running experiment in Colorado provides an ‘alarming’ view of how rapidly unchecked global heating could transform fragile ecosystemsEvery summer, people descend on the wildflower capital of Colorado to see grasslands flush with corn lilies, aspen sunflowers and sub-alpine larkspur. In January 1991, scientists set up a unique experiment in these Rocky Mountain meadows. It was one of the first (and longest running) to work out how the changing climate would affect an ecosystem.At the time,

summary.phenomenon: In a long-running experiment conducted in the Rocky Mountain meadows of Colorado since January 1991, scientists observed 'alarming' changes when they heated wildflower meadows by 2 degrees Celsius.
summary.cause: This experiment was conducted by simulating global warming to understand how the changing climate affects ecosystems.
summary.outlook: This experiment demonstrates that unchecked global heating can rapidly transform fragile ecosystems.
